narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70, which stands out well above the typical 3.21 average, along with an LSAT score of 37 that's right in line with the usual 36.70 mark for students in this dataset. Your background includes middle family income and plans to attend part-time, which is less common since about 93% of similar students go full-time. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 55.2% chance you'll fail the bar on your first try—enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application, as the school prioritizes those predicted to pass with high confidence. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in school wasn't as competitive as it needs to be for bar success. This outweighed the positive pushes from your strong undergraduate GPA, which worked strongly in your favor by suggesting solid foundational skills, and your LSAT score, which also nudged things toward a passing prediction. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your lower Year 3 decile is a standout concern, as higher rankings there correlate strongly with first-try success in the model's view. Your part-time status and middle-income background played smaller roles but didn't shift the balance much. While this rejection stings, especially with your impressive undergrad record, it highlights where the model sees risk based on patterns from thousands of past students. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic decile through targeted improvements in later-year performance, perhaps by seeking tutoring or study groups tailored to law school prep. Retaking the LSAT for a slight edge or documenting reasons for your Year 3 decile could also help in appeals or re-applications. Many students in similar situations turn this around by addressing these gaps head-on, and with your GPA as a foundation, you're well-positioned to do just that. Keep pushing forward—law school doors can reopen with the right adjustments.
